name: SIMD

x.executionModel: A single instruction applies the same operation across every lane of a vector register (e.g. 8× float32 in a 256-bit AVX register), within one core.
x.dataOrg: Wants contiguous, aligned, Structure-of-Arrays data so adjacent lanes read adjacent elements; AoS layouts force gather/scatter that erodes the benefit.
x.hwConstraints: Register width and instruction set vary by target (SSE / AVX / AVX-512 / NEON / SVE); alignment and tail handling are required, and AVX-512 can downclock the core.
x.compilerSupport: Auto-vectorisation (fragile), OpenMP simd pragmas, portable wrappers (std::simd), or hand-written platform intrinsics.
x.portability: Intrinsics are ISA-specific; portable-SIMD layers trade some peak throughput for cross-target code.
x.expectedBenefit: Typically 2–16× on arithmetic-dense, branch-light kernels with good layout; near zero or negative otherwise.
bottlenecks: Memory bandwidth on low arithmetic-intensity kernels
Setup and tail overhead on short arrays
divergenceRisks: Data-dependent branches force masking/blending or serialise lanes
x.workloadShapes: Dense numeric loops, image/audio/video codecs, physics, ML kernels, columnar database scans.

def: Single Instruction, Multiple Data: one instruction operates on a vector of lanes simultaneously.
problem: Increase arithmetic throughput on regular, data-parallel work without more threads.
mechanism: Vector registers (SSE/AVX/NEON) hold N elements; a single op processes all lanes. Requires aligned, contiguous, SoA-friendly data and uniform control flow.
failures: SoA refactor required; AoS data stalls
Slower than scalar on tiny/irregular data
whenNot: Tiny datasets where setup cost dominates
myths: "SIMD = multithreading" — it is per-core data parallelism, orthogonal to threads
"The compiler always vectorises" — it often cannot prove safety
